summary: Cantrill discovered a mysterious tweet posted from his account that he didn't create, initially assuming he'd been hacked. After investigating the tweet's metadata, he found it contained media from a BBC journalist's tweet about Berlin, with no apparent malicious intent. He concludes this wasn't a hack but likely a software bug—either in Twitter's client or server—and leaves the tweet up as evidence for debugging.
key_points: A tweet appeared on Cantrill's account that he didn't post, triggering immediate security response
The tweet contained only images from another user's post about Berlin campaign posters with no caption or malicious content
Analysis of the tweet's JSON metadata revealed the media was sourced from a BBC editor's tweet
The lack of malicious intent and bizarre nature of the content suggests a software bug rather than a hack
Cantrill questions whether Twitter's client or server had a bug causing tweets to be created with others' media
money_quotes: There was just one problem: I didn't tweet it.
Why would someone who had just hacked my account burn it by tweeting an innocuous (if idiosyncratic) photo of campaign posters on the streets of Berlin?!
Suddenly this is feeling less like I've been hacked, and more like I'm the victim of data corruption.

I'm leaving the tweet up there in hopes that it will provide some clues – and with the belief that the villain in the story, if ever brought to justice, will be a member of the shadowy cabal that I have fought my entire career: busted software.
key_insight: When something inexplicable happens with software, always consider that bugs and data corruption may be more likely culprits than malicious actors.
